questions.question: What does the 'try' block do in a try/catch statement?
options: B) Contains code that might throw an exception

questions.question: What type does the 'catch' parameter have by default in TypeScript?
When does the 'finally' block execute?
options: A) Only when an error occurs
B) Only when no error occurs
C) Always, regardless of whether an error occurred

questions.question: How do you create a custom error class in TypeScript?
options: B) class MyError extends Error { constructor(msg: string) { super(msg); this.name = 'MyError'; } }
C) type MyError = Error & { code: number }
D) function MyError(msg: string) { return new Error(msg); }

questions.question: Why should you set this.name in a custom error class?
options: B) So the error can be serialized to JSON
C) So the error type is identifiable in stack traces and instanceof checks
D) To prevent the error from being caught

questions.question: What is the Result type pattern?
options: B) A discriminated union representing either a successful value (Ok) or an error (Err), avoiding exceptions for control flow
C) A Promise that always resolves
D) A type guard for error checking

questions.question: What is an advantage of the Result pattern over try/catch?
options: B) Errors are part of the type signature, forcing callers to handle both cases
C) It automatically retries on failure
D) It can catch async errors

questions.question: What does a mapResult function typically do?
options: B) Transforms the success value of a Result while passing through errors unchanged
C) Maps an array of Results to an array of values
D) Creates a new Result type

questions.question: Why is it important to check if a caught value is an Error instance?
options: A) Because only Error objects can be logged
B) Because anything can be thrown in JavaScript, not just Error objects
C) Because TypeScript requires it for compilation
D) Because non-Error throws crash the program
B) 'Not an Error: just a string'

questions.question: How do you handle errors in an async function?
options: C) With try/catch inside the async function or .catch() on the returned Promise
D) Async functions cannot throw errors

questions.question: What happens if an async function throws and there is no catch handler?
options: A) The error is silently ignored
B) An unhandled promise rejection occurs
D) The error is caught by the global scope

questions.question: What is the purpose of re-throwing an error?
options: A) To convert it to a different error type
B) To log it and let it propagate to a higher-level handler

questions.question: What is a 'wrapping' error pattern?
options: A) Catching an error and throwing a new error that includes the original as a cause
D) Using finally to wrap up operations

questions.question: What does the Error 'cause' property (ES2022) allow you to do?
options: B) Chain errors by attaching the original error as the cause of a new error
C) Prevent the error from propagating
D) Log errors to a file
B) Swallowing errors silently makes debugging impossible
D) You must specify the error type

questions.question: When using the Result pattern, what should you return for an async operation?
Why is 'finally' useful for resource cleanup?
options: A) It runs faster than catch
B) It executes whether or not an error occurred, ensuring cleanup always happens
C) It prevents errors from propagating
D) It automatically closes file handles
